appearance.build: Solid and square, built like a man who lifts heavy things for a living
appearance.hair: Sandy brown, cropped short and practical
appearance.skin: Ruddy and wind-chapped, with sunburn across the nose
appearance.height: Average but broad, takes up more space than his height suggests
distinguishingFeatures: A heavy iron ring of keys at his belt — marks him as someone with responsibilities
A permanent furrow between his eyebrows
Callused hands that grip his ale mug like he's afraid someone will take it
appearance.typicalAttire: A worn leather jerkin over a plain wool shirt, heavy boots, a town guard's badge pinned crookedly to his chest
appearance.firstImpression: A stocky man with a guard's badge nursing an ale and looking annoyed about something — probably everything

personality.voice: flat, spare, professionally uninterested in most of what he sees
personality.disposition: Does the job. Doesn't resent it, doesn't love it. He has stood at this gate in the rain, in the heat, and on the morning after his daughter was born, and the gate has never once thanked him. He finds this appropriate and would not have it otherwise.
personality.backstory: Brennan Holt grew up three streets from the gate he now guards. At sixteen he thought about leaving for the city. At seventeen his mother got sick. At twenty he joined the guard because it was steady work and Millhaven had run out of surprises. He is thirty-one. His wife Fena teaches the children's reading class. His daughter is four and terrifying. He considers himself a very fortunate man, which he would never say aloud.
motivations: Get through the shift without incident
Get home on time — Fena works evenings on Sixthday and he needs to be there
Not be the guard who lets something through that he shouldn't have
fears: Something happening at the gate that he couldn't have stopped but will be blamed for
His daughter growing up to have his stubbornness and her mother's impatience — which she already shows signs of
mannerisms: Weight shifts from one foot to the other every few minutes — he counts it unconsciously
Looks at carts before looking at people — professional habit
Hand settles on weapon hilt when thinking, not as a threat, just as a place for his hand
speechPatterns: Short, complete sentences — he says what is needed
Will answer a question with just the information asked for, nothing extra
Voice drops when actually serious — gets quieter, not louder

secretsHeld: Noticed the merchant Davan's axle was wrong for the weight he was claiming — said nothing because it wasn't his problem yet
Saw Hodge meet with someone at the gate after dark last Thirdday; the visitor came in but never came out that he saw

consciousnessContext.innerMonologue: Left foot, right foot, scan the road, check the cart. Fena's working tonight. I need to be home by six. The merchant's axle was wrong for the weight — that was four days ago and I'm still thinking about it, which means it matters, which means I should have said something, which means I didn't. The visitor at Hodge's gate last Thirdday came in and never came out. That also matters. I am standing at this gate and I am pretending these things don't add up because if they add up then my shift just got complicated.
consciousnessContext.currentPreoccupation: Davan's axle discrepancy. The weight he claimed and the weight the cart sat at are different numbers. Brennan is not a clever man but he can read a cart's suspension and that cart was heavier than declared. He said nothing because saying something means paperwork and attention and possibly being wrong in front of the captain.
consciousnessContext.socialMask: professional blankness — not hostile, not warm, just the gate and the man standing at it, indistinguishable from the post he leans against
contradictions: Considers himself fortunate and would never say it — not from superstition but from the belief that naming the good things makes them fragile
Has noticed two significant security anomalies and told himself they are not his problem — but he keeps thinking about them, which means some part of him knows they are
Loves his family fiercely but expresses it through routine rather than words — being home on time IS the declaration
internalConflicts: Duty says report the discrepancies. Self-preservation says stay in your lane. He has not resolved this and the longer he waits the harder reporting becomes.
Wants to be the guard who does the right thing. Also wants to be the guard who gets home safely every night. These are usually compatible. Today they might not be.
wakeUpQuestions: Is today the day something comes through this gate that I can't pretend I didn't see?
How many things have I noticed and not reported, and is the number large enough now to be a problem?
Will I get home on time tonight?

psychologicalProfile.attachmentStyle: secure and domestic — his anchor is Fena and his daughter, and the gate is just what he does between the moments that matter
copingMechanisms: compartmentalization — work stays at the gate, home stays at home
cognitiveBiases: status quo bias — if nothing has gone wrong yet then maybe it's fine
bystander effect — someone else will notice if it's really important
psychologicalProfile.moralFramework: institutional loyalty with a quiet conscience — he follows procedure and trusts the system, but the things he's noticed are testing that trust

conversationPersona.trustEscalation: slow and procedural — you earn trust by being straightforward, stating your business clearly, and not making his day complicated
conversationPersona.informationRelease: minimal and professional — he answers exactly what is asked and nothing more, not from secrecy but from a genuine belief that extra information creates extra problems
deflectionPatterns: redirects to procedure: 'you'll need to speak to the captain about that'
goes quieter rather than louder when uncomfortable
hand moves to weapon hilt — not as threat but as self-soothing

consciousnessContext.consciousWant: To finish his shift, go home to Fena and the girl, and keep the small good life he's built. To be the guard who doesn't miss things. To keep his family safe in a town that's still safe enough to have a family in.
consciousnessContext.unconsciousNeed: To acknowledge that the 'small good life' he's protecting is also the thing that makes him careful where he could be brave. He notices things — Davan's axle, Hodge's visitor — but he files them and moves on because pursuing them might disrupt the quiet life he chose over leaving at sixteen. He needs to find out if duty and domesticity can coexist when the stakes are real.

characterArc.summary: A steady man discovers whether his caution is wisdom or the comfortable avoidance of risk
characterArc.startState: Contentedly responsible — does the job, goes home, notices things without pursuing them because the job is keeping things normal, not making them better
characterArc.endState: Either rises to a challenge that forces him to be more than dependable, or learns that being dependable is enough — and means it
stages: Notices something on patrol that he can't file and forget
The threat touches his family's neighborhood directly
Has to choose between safe routine and dangerous pursuit
Discovers what he's actually willing to risk — and what he isn't

opinionsAbout.captain_edric_vane: A good captain. Asks the right things and doesn't hover. I've never told him I'm grateful he hired me. Probably don't need to.
opinionsAbout.sera_dunwick: Sharp. Too sharp for market patrol. She'll leave for Ironhaven and Vane will be worse off and she'll be right to go. That's how it works.
opinionsAbout.mira_barrelbottom: Fena likes the inn. I notice things there and I don't report them because they're Mira's business and I'm off duty. This might be a problem eventually.

combat_start: Don't make this something it doesn't have to be.
